skill.name: react-performance
skill.description: Performance optimization for React web applications. Use when optimizing renders, implementing virtualization, memoizing components, or debugging performance issues.
skill.summary: Guides React render, list, bundle, and debugging optimizations for faster web apps.
skill.author: CJHarmath

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and medium risk patterns, but review found they are documentation examples in SKILL.md rather than executable skill code. Dynamic import, Web Worker, and process.env examples are legitimate React performance guidance, and the external command, weak crypto, reconnaissance, and environment file findings are false positives. No prompt injection, data exfiltration, network access, or malicious execution intent was found.

low_findings.title: Dynamic Import Examples Are Documentation Only
low_findings.description: The dynamic import() calls are static React.lazy examples for code splitting with local page modules. They are not executed by the skill and do not load user-controlled paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The imports use fixed local paths inside a Markdown TypeScript example. No executable skill file or user-controlled import target is present.

low_findings.title: Web Worker Example Uses Local Worker File
low_findings.description: The Worker constructor appears in a React performance example that moves heavy computation off the main thread. It points to a local worker.ts file and includes cleanup with terminate().
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code is contained in documentation and uses a local URL pattern common in Vite and modern bundlers. There is no network worker source or persistence behavior.

low_findings.title: Development Environment Check Is Benign
low_findings.description: The process.env.NODE_ENV access is a development-only guard for why-did-you-render setup. It does not read secrets and does not access an environment file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only environment access shown is NODE_ENV, a non-secret runtime mode flag. The surrounding example conditionally loads a development diagnostic package.

low_findings.title: External Command Findings Are Markdown Fence False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer interpreted Markdown code fences as Ruby or shell backtick execution. These locations are documentation examples, not shell execution calls.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged backticks are Markdown delimiters around TypeScript and JSX snippets. No shell command, Ruby code, or command execution API is present.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm and system reconnaissance alerts match ordinary prose, alt text, or property names in React examples. No hashing, cryptography, hostname, user, or system discovery code is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found React component names, item identifiers, image alt text, and skill description text at these locations. None of the lines contain crypto or reconnaissance behavior.

content.value_statement: React apps often slow down because of unnecessary renders, large lists, and expensive UI work. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through focused React performance fixes.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use useMemo, useCallback, and React.memo.
Shows list virtualization patterns with fixed and variable row heights.
Guides Zustand selector optimization to reduce avoidable renders.
Covers lazy loading, code splitting, debouncing, and throttling patterns.
Provides React DevTools Profiler and why-did-you-render debugging steps.
Recommends web workers for heavy client-side computations.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples, not an automated profiler.
It does not measure runtime performance without project data.
It focuses on React web applications, not React Native.
It assumes users can adapt TypeScript examples to their project.

use_cases.title: Reduce Product List Lag
use_cases.description: Find virtualization, memoization, and callback stability improvements for large React lists.

use_cases.title: Debug Re-Render Problems
use_cases.description: Use profiler checks and selector patterns to identify components that render too often.

use_cases.title: Plan Performance Reviews
use_cases.description: Create a checklist for code splitting, image loading, expensive computations, and release readiness.

prompt_templates.title: Find Basic Render Issues
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a slow component
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this React component for avoidable re-renders. Focus on memoization, stable props, and simple fixes first.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize a Large List
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this React list view. Recommend when to use virtualization, memoized row components, and stable item callbacks.

prompt_templates.title: Profile State Updates
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect these Zustand selectors and render paths. Identify broad subscriptions, unstable derived values, and profiler checks to confirm the fix.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Performance Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a React performance plan for this app. Cover route splitting, image loading, web workers, render profiling, and release validation.

output_examples.input: A product grid slows down when scrolling through hundreds of items.
output: Virtualize the scrolling area so only visible rows render.
Memoize item cards if their props are stable.
Use stable selection callbacks to avoid re-rendering every row.
Confirm the result with the React DevTools Profiler.

output_examples.input: A dashboard re-renders whenever any global store value changes.
output: Replace whole-store subscriptions with narrow selectors.
Use shallow comparison when selecting multiple values.
Move derived data into memoized selectors or component memoization.
Measure before and after renders with profiling tools.

output_examples.input: A route has a slow first render and large bundle cost.
output: Split heavy routes so they load only when needed.
Lazy load images below the initial viewport.
Move expensive client-side processing away from urgent rendering.
Track improvements with bundle and interaction measurements.

best_practices: Profile before making broad memoization changes.
Keep props, callbacks, and selectors stable where child renders are expensive.
Use virtualization or deferred work for large data and heavy computations.
anti_patterns: Memoizing simple values where the memoization cost is higher than recalculation.
Subscribing React components to entire global stores when they need one field.
Rendering large lists directly without virtualization or pagination.

faq.question: Does this skill automatically profile my app?
faq.answer: No. It gives profiling workflows and optimization patterns for an AI assistant to apply with your project context.

faq.question: Is React.memo always recommended?
faq.answer: No. It helps most when props are stable and the component is expensive or renders often.

faq.question: Can it help with Zustand performance?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes selector and shallow comparison patterns that reduce unnecessary component updates.

faq.question: Does it cover code splitting?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains lazy loading routes and components with Suspense-based patterns.

faq.question: Can it optimize image loading?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers browser lazy loading, IntersectionObserver patterns, and Next.js Image usage.

faq.question: Is this skill for React Native?
faq.answer: No. It is written for React web applications and browser performance patterns.
